gcmotion.Particle#

class gcmotion.Particle(species: str, mu: float, init_cond: array, tspan: array, R: float, a: float, q: QFactor, Bfield: MagneticField, Efield: ElectricField, method: str = 'RK45', rtol: float = 1e-05)#

Initializes a particle, which calculates the orbit, orbit type, unit convertion factors, and motion frequencies.

Supposedly there is no need to change anything here. Electric Fields and Q factors should be changed in the respective gcmotion/*.py files.

__init__(species: str, mu: float, init_cond: array, tspan: array, R: float, a: float, q: QFactor, Bfield: MagneticField, Efield: ElectricField, method: str = 'RK45', rtol: float = 1e-05)#

Initializes particle and grabs configuration.

Parameters:

  • species (str) – the particle species, used to later set charge and mass automatically (from gcmotion/config.yaml)

  • mu (float) – magnetic moment

  • init_cond (np.array) – 1x4 initial conditions array (later, self.init_cond includes 2 more initial conditions, [\(\theta_0, \psi_0, \psi_{p0}, \zeta_0, P_{\zeta_0}\)])

  • tspan (np.array) – The ODE interval, in [\(t_0, t_f\), steps]

  • R (float) – The tokamak’s major radius in [m].

  • a (float) – The tokamak’s minor radius in [m].

  • q (QFactor) – Qfactor object that supports query methods for getting values of \(q(\psi)\) and \(\psi_p(\psi)\).

  • Bfield (MagneticField) – Magnetic Field Object that supports query methods for getting values for the field itself. magnitude (in [T]) of the magnetic field B, as in \([g, I, B_0]\).

  • Efield (ElectricField) – Electric Field Object that supports query methods for getting values for the field itself and some derivatives of its potential.

  • method (str) – Method used by the solver. Can be either ‘lsoda’ or ‘RK45’. Defaults to ‘RK45’.

  • rtol (float) – Relative tolerance of the RK45 solver. Defaults to \(10^{-6}\).

Methods

__init__(species, mu, init_cond, tspan, R, ...)

Initializes particle and grabs configuration.

freq_analysis(angle[, trim, normal, ...])

Given the input angle, runs Frequncy Analysis and prints results.

q_kinetic()

Calculates the \(q_{kinetic}\) factor.

run([info, orbit])

Calculates the motion and attributes of the particle.
run(info: bool = True, orbit=True)#

Calculates the motion and attributes of the particle.

This is the function that must be called after the initial conditions are set. It runs the needed methods in the correct order first and initializes the “Plot” class afterwards, which contains all the plotting methods.

Parameters:

info (bool, optional) – Whether or not to print the particle’s calculated attributes. Defaults to True.

_orbit()#

Calculates the orbit of the particle, as well as \(P_\theta\) and \(\psi_p\).

Calculates the time evolution of the dynamical variables \(\theta, \psi, \zeta, \rho_{||}\). Afterwards, it calculates the canonical momenta \(P_\theta\) and \(P_\zeta\), and the poloidal flux \(\psi_p\) through the q factor.

The orbit can be calculated with SciPy’s solve_ivp or odeint methods. The default is solve_ivp (RK4(5)).

The solve_ivp function integrates an ODE system using a 4th order Runge-Kutta method, with an error estimation of 5th order (RK4(5)). The default relative tolerance is \(10^{-6}\).

The odeint function integrates an ODE system using lsoda from the FORTRAN library odepack.

Orbit is stored in “self”.

_conversion_factors()#

Calculates the conversion coeffecient needed to convert from lab to NU and vice versa.

_energies()#

Calculates the particle’s energy in [NU], [eV] and [J], using its initial conditions.

_orbit_type()#

Estimates the orbit type given the initial conditions ONLY.

Caution

This method works only in the absence of an Electric Field.

Trapped/passing: The particle is trapped if rho vanishes, so we can check if rho changes sign. Since \(\rho = \dfrac{\sqrt{2W-2\mu B}}{B}\), we need only to check under the root.

Confined/lost: (from shape page 87) We only have to check if the particle is in-between the 2 left parabolas.

freq_analysis(angle: str, trim: bool = True, normal: bool = False, remove_bias: bool = True, info: bool = True)#

Given the input angle, runs Frequncy Analysis and prints results.

Trimming and removing the bias of the timeseries gives the best results, both in peak-to-peak calculation of the frequency and FFT.

Calling this functions also stores the 0th and 1st frequency of the angle, which are needed to calculate the q_kinetic.

Parameters:

  • angle (str) – The angle to analyse,

  • trim (bool, optional) – Whether or not to trim the edges of the time series. Defaults to True.

  • normal (bool, optional) – Whether or not to use normal or lab units. Defaults to True.

  • remove_bias (bool, optional) – Whether or not to remove the signal’s bias. Defaults to True.

  • info (bool, optional) – Whether or not to print results. Defaults to True.

Returns:

The results of the analysis.

Return type:

str

q_kinetic()#

Calculates the \(q_{kinetic}\) factor.

Also checks if the frequencies have been calculated in the best way possible. If not, they are re-calculated.

Returns:

The calculation results.

Return type:

str
